messages.author: UP
messages.text: There are more local postings for Georgia, Tenesse, Virginia, Louisiana, etc, and soon it will be better for most people to go to those groups to find their neighbors and get help connecting. So, look for a group for your state or local area, and join that too if it exists. If a more local entry for your particular part of the SE USA doesn't exist yet, please consider creating it. At the moment of posting this, however, there don't seem to be many of us yet, and I hope that this can be a helpfull place for interstate dialogue and coordinating longer distance connectivity as we grow. One more recommendation: since different coding rates should be interoperable, so I'm going to suggest we set that parameter based on regional mesh density. If you don't have meshcore neighbors, set cr8. If your mesh takes off, move back to cr5. (edited)
messages.content_html: There are more local postings for Georgia, Tenesse, Virginia, Louisiana, etc, and soon it will be better for most people to go to those groups to find their neighbors and get help connecting. So, look for a group for your state or local area, and join that too if it exists. If a more local entry for your particular part of the SE USA doesn't exist yet, please consider creating it. At the moment of posting this, however, there don't seem to be many of us yet, and I hope that this can be a helpfull place for interstate dialogue and coordinating longer distance connectivity as we grow. One more recommendation: since different coding rates should be interoperable, so I'm going to suggest we set that parameter based on regional mesh density. If you don't have meshcore neighbors, set cr8. If your mesh takes off, move back to cr5. (edited)

messages.author: UP
messages.text: Here's a quick and very simple example from my elevation visualization workflow with tailor-made KML files in Google Earth. in this image, no blue shading = 300+ft elevation, 1 blue layer = 200-300 ft, 2 layers = under 200 ft. It's way more powerful in urban places where the trees and buildings are 3d modeled, but I'd be happy to snap specific views or share some KMLs if it would prove useful.
messages.content_html: Here's a quick and very simple example from my elevation visualization workflow with tailor-made KML files in Google Earth. in this image, no blue shading = 300+ft elevation, 1 blue layer = 200-300 ft, 2 layers = under 200 ft. It's way more powerful in urban places where the trees and buildings are 3d modeled, but I'd be happy to snap specific views or share some KMLs if it would prove useful.

messages.author: UP
messages.text: I need to stop procrastinating, bite the bullet, and buy hardware, so it will be here in time to start deploying before all the leaves are off the trees. I like assembling gadgets, and love hiking, but my wallet is nervous. Planning to visit: Stone mt, Kennesaw mt, Tray mt, Sawnee mt, Dowdell Knob, Paris Mt. Also trying to identify an accessible site that would give coverage in SW ATL. (edited)

messages.author: UP
messages.text: I have some schemes in the works for extending to into North Carolina and/or Alabama.... possibly even chasing a distance record, but I'm trying to start with being a little more practical for my local Atlanta and north GA, and then we'll see how the budget and network look after a few routers are online.
messages.content_html: I have some schemes in the works for extending to into North Carolina and/or Alabama.... possibly even chasing a distance record, but I'm trying to start with being a little more practical for my local Atlanta and north GA, and then we'll see how the budget and network look after a few routers are online.

messages.reply_html:
UP
Edited initial post to recomend coding rate of 8 for areas that are just getting off the ground.

messages.author: UP
messages.text: Yes. My understanding is that cr is not a receive parameter at all, and only effects the transmit side. Regardless, I have now tested communication between a node with cr 5 and one with cr 8, and can now confirm that it works. (edited)
messages.content_html: Yes. My understanding is that cr is not a receive parameter at all, and only effects the transmit side. Regardless, I have now tested communication between a node with cr 5 and one with cr 8, and can now confirm that it works. (edited)

embeds_html:
TennMesh
TennMesh is a community-driven MeshCore project building an off-grid communication network across East Tennessee. We’re working to establish reliable MeshCore coverage from Chattanooga to Knoxville, using open-source software and LoRa radios to create a resilient, decentralized mesh network that works even when cell to

messages.author: Protos - WØGOD
messages.text: First time you 'turn it on' it needs power. It can work from the solar but that will take longer. Hooking up to usb is the best. Especially since you need to flash MeshCore. Congrats on the new repeater ! Stock antenna is good, but there are much better available. I went with a Alpha Networks 5 db omni.
messages.content_html: First time you 'turn it on' it needs power. It can work from the solar but that will take longer. Hooking up to usb is the best. Especially since you need to flash MeshCore. Congrats on the new repeater ! Stock antenna is good, but there are much better available. I went with a Alpha Networks 5 db omni.

messages.author: Protos - WØGOD
messages.text: Woody make sure to Erase Flash first before loading MeshCore. It comes with Meshtastic as you know, so best to wipe that clean and have a nice fresh base for MeshCore.

messages.author: UP
messages.text: It's taking me a while to catch up, but it sounds like for now, adding your state to the router regions using the naming format "us-va" for Virginia, "us-ga" for Georgia, etc, is a good start. If your coverage area spans multiple states or you are near a line, it sounds like adding all the potentially apliccable ones is the way to go. (edited)
messages.content_html: It's taking me a while to catch up, but it sounds like for now, adding your state to the router regions using the naming format "us-va" for Virginia, "us-ga" for Georgia, etc, is a good start. If your coverage area spans multiple states or you are near a line, it sounds like adding all the potentially apliccable ones is the way to go. (edited)

It would be nice to cover the big trail and pass messages along it.

messages.author: UP
messages.text: Seems like an extremely niche and restrictive region. With any proposed region, think: how much volume of traffic could this keep from having to be sent out over a vastly larger area? Also there's the problem of ensuring the folks deploying repeaters know about all the regions, otherwise traffic gets dropped and people find the region unreliable. I could see more of an argument for something like us-eastern, as it could have lots of other uses. We should definitely be talking to Boston folks about stuff like that, though. They might already have something in the field.
messages.content_html: Seems like an extremely niche and restrictive region. With any proposed region, think: how much volume of traffic could this keep from having to be sent out over a vastly larger area? Also there's the problem of ensuring the folks deploying repeaters know about all the regions, otherwise traffic gets dropped and people find the region unreliable. I could see more of an argument for something like us-eastern, as it could have lots of other uses. We should definitely be talking to Boston folks about stuff like that, though. They might already have something in the field.
messages.reply_html:
Fidonet127
What about something like us-trail-at for the trail?

messages.author: Fidonet127
messages.text: My thoughts were that it would enable communication along the entire trail, would help in state boundary areas and people wouldn’t have to keep switching regions, which might be hard to do on the trail.
messages.content_html: My thoughts were that it would enable communication along the entire trail, would help in state boundary areas and people wouldn’t have to keep switching regions, which might be hard to do on the trail.
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: UP
messages.text: I think what I was trying to convey got misunderstood. There are almost certainly other use cases where having a eastern states region would be useful, and we probably should have one. What I think would be harmful is a long list of similar yet distinct regions named for specific use cases. Something like a "us-eastern" region could be used by an AT hikers channel, and also used for a channel sharing hurricane information across states, as well as many other things that have nothing to do with the trail. My argument isn't against a several state region, but that more thought be put into making regions general purpose, rather than naming one for each use case. Another concern I had reading the suggestion a first was that lines of sight around the trail are wildly variable, and routes are likely to need to leave the trail area, especially over longer distances. Does anyone here know of already existing threads or groups dedicated to national-scale coordination, like what larger-than-state regions should be called and how to tailor them to be the most useful to everyone? (edited)
messages.content_html:
I think what I was trying to convey got misunderstood.
  • There are almost certainly other use cases where having a eastern states region would be useful, and we probably should have one. What I think would be harmful is a long list of similar yet distinct regions named for specific use cases.
  • Something like a "us-eastern" region could be used by an AT hikers channel, and also used for a channel sharing hurricane information across states, as well as many other things that have nothing to do with the trail. My argument isn't against a several state region, but that more thought be put into making regions general purpose, rather than naming one for each use case.
  • Another concern I had reading the suggestion a first was that lines of sight around the trail are wildly variable, and routes are likely to need to leave the trail area, especially over longer distances.
  • Does anyone here know of already existing threads or groups dedicated to national-scale coordination, like what larger-than-state regions should be called and how to tailor them to be the most useful to everyone?
 (edited)

messages.author: JWclutCh007
messages.text: As long as the default >* F rule is left in place you can add many region restrictions and every packet will still repeat. The only time that a packet would be restricted is if the sender applies a region scope to a channel they are using. Public by default has no region and will always flood.
messages.content_html: As long as the default >* F rule is left in place you can add many region restrictions and every packet will still repeat. The only time that a packet would be restricted is if the sender applies a region scope to a channel they are using. Public by default has no region and will always flood.
messages.reactions_html: 2

I’m not arguing against anything. Just a thought process and I am trying to figure things out. You have good ideas. I also don’t want traffic unnecessarily dropped.
